questions.question: Differentiate between continuous-time and discrete-time signals with examples.
keyPoints: CT signal: defined for all t, example: sin(t), cos(ωt)
DT signal: defined only at discrete instants, example: x[n] = sin(ωn)
DT usually obtained from CT by sampling at t = nT

questions.question: State and explain the properties of a linear system.
keyPoints: Additivity: T{x₁ + x₂} = T{x₁} + T{x₂}
Homogeneity: T{ax} = aT{x} for any scalar a
Combined: Superposition - response to sum equals sum of responses
Linearity implies zero output for zero input

questions.question: Define unit step and unit impulse functions and explain their relationship.
keyPoints: Unit step u(t): 0 for t<0, 1 for t≥0 - represents sudden application of constant signal
Unit impulse δ(t): infinite at t=0, zero elsewhere, area = 1 - represents instantaneous impulse
Relationship: δ(t) = du(t)/dt and u(t) = ∫δ(τ)dτ from -∞ to t
Impulse is derivative of step, step is integral of impulse

questions.question: Explain time-shifting, time-scaling, and time-flipping operations on signals.
keyPoints: Time-shifting: x(t-t₀) shifts signal right by t₀ (if t₀>0)
Time-scaling: x(at) compresses by a if a>1, expands if 0<a<1
Time-flipping: x(-t) reflects signal about y-axis (horizontal flip)
These operations affect signal geometry without changing fundamental characteristics

questions.question: Define energy and power signals with examples and mathematical conditions.
keyPoints: Energy signal: E = ∫|x(t)|²dt is finite and E>0, P = 0 (example: rectangular pulse)
Power signal: P = lim(1/T)∫|x(t)|²dt is finite non-zero, E = ∞ (example: sinusoid)
Neither: E = 0 and P = 0 (zero signal)
A signal cannot be both energy and power signal simultaneously

questions.question: State the difference between causal and non-causal systems with examples.
keyPoints: Causal: output depends only on present and past inputs, h(t)=0 for t<0 (example: y[n]=x[n]+x[n-1])
Non-causal: output depends on future inputs, h(t)≠0 for t<0 (example: ideal filter with future samples)
Real-time physical systems must be causal
Non-causal systems can be realized offline with stored data

questions.question: Explain the commutative, associative, and distributive properties of convolution.
keyPoints: Commutative: x(t)*h(t) = h(t)*x(t), order doesn't matter
Associative: [x(t)*h(t)]*g(t) = x(t)*[h(t)*g(t)]
Distributive: x(t)*[h(t)+g(t)] = x(t)*h(t) + x(t)*g(t)
These properties simplify system analysis and implementation

questions.question: Define convolution integral and explain how to compute output of an LTI system using convolution. Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Convolution: y(t) = x(t)*h(t) = ∫x(τ)h(t-τ)dτ from -∞ to ∞
For LTI systems, output is convolution of input with impulse response
Steps: (1) Express both signals as function of τ (2) Flip one signal (3) Shift by t (4) Multiply and integrate overlapping regions
Example: If x(t) = u(t) and h(t) = e^(-at)u(t), then y(t) = ∫₀ᵗe^(-a(t-τ))dτ = (1/a)(1-e^(-at))u(t)
Graphical interpretation helps visualize the sliding and overlapping process

questions.question: Explain the conditions for a system to be linear, time-invariant, causal, and stable. How are these properties related to impulse response?
keyPoints: Linear: satisfies superposition (additivity + homogeneity)
Time-invariant: shifting input shifts output by same amount
Causal: h(t) = 0 for t < 0
Stable (BIBO): ∫|h(t)|dt < ∞ (absolutely integrable)
Impulse response h(t) completely characterizes LTI system; all properties derived from it
For LTI: linearity+time-invariance → convolution; causality → h(t) one-sided; stability → |h(t)| integrable

questions.question: Derive the relationship between Fourier Transform and Laplace Transform. Explain regions of convergence.
keyPoints: Laplace Transform: X(s) = ∫x(t)e^(-st)dt where s = σ + jω
Fourier Transform: X(ω) = ∫x(t)e^(-jωt)dt
Fourier Transform is Laplace Transform evaluated at s = jω (along imaginary axis)
ROCs: For causal signals, ROC is Re(s) > α (right-half plane)
For anti-causal, ROC is Re(s) < α (left-half plane)
ROC must include jω axis for Fourier Transform to exist
Signals with poles on jω axis (e.g., sinusoids) don't have Fourier Transform in strict sense

questions.question: Explain the classification of signals into periodic and aperiodic, even and odd. Prove that any signal can be decomposed into even and odd components.
keyPoints: Periodic: x(t+T) = x(t) for all t, T is fundamental period; otherwise aperiodic
Even: x(-t) = x(t); Odd: x(-t) = -x(t)
Proof: Let x_e(t) = (x(t)+x(-t))/2 and x_o(t) = (x(t)-x(-t))/2
Then x_e(t) + x_o(t) = (x(t)+x(-t)+x(t)-x(-t))/2 = x(t)
Also x_e(-t) = (x(-t)+x(t))/2 = x_e(t) - even
Any signal is sum of unique even and odd components
